notes: Tracks planned and in-progress features for the Hearth agent harness. Mark passes=true only after manual end-to-end verification. Do not remove or rename entries — add new ones at the bottom of the relevant category. Priorities: 1=highest, 3=lowest.

features.title: Budget analytics — monthly spending by category
features.description: A /budget/analytics page showing a breakdown of spending per category for the current month, with a month-picker to view historical months. Bar or pie chart. Uses existing transaction + category data.
steps: Navigate to /budget
Click 'Analytics' or a month-picker control
See a chart of spending by category for the selected month
Verify totals match the transaction list for that month
Switch months and confirm data updates correctly

features.title: Budget export — download transactions as CSV
features.description: A button on the main budget page that exports all transactions (or filtered transactions) as a downloadable CSV file. Include columns: date, merchant, amount, category, notes.
steps: Navigate to /budget
Click 'Export CSV'
A file downloads named hearth-budget-YYYY-MM.csv
Open the file and verify columns: date, merchant, amount, category, notes
Verify all visible transactions are present

features.title: Budget dashboard widget
features.description: A new dashboard widget showing this month's total spending vs a budget target, with a simple progress bar. Widget is addable via the Add Widget sheet. Config: optional monthly budget target amount.
steps: Navigate to the dashboard
Click 'Add widget' and find 'Budget' in the list
Add the widget
See current month's spending and an optional budget target bar
Widget reloads with fresh data on each visit

features.title: Category groups — collapsible category sections
features.description:
Allow budget categories to be organized into named groups. Groups are collapsible in the UI. Each category belongs to one group; ungrouped categories fall into a default 'Other' bucket. Requires a new budget_category_groups table with a sort_order and name, and a group_id FK on budget_categories. The /budget/categories response should nest categories inside their group. On first setup, seed the default groups and assignments listed below.

Proposed default groups (inspired by YNAB, adapted for Hearth):

Group 1 — Fixed Monthly (sort 1): Immediate recurring obligations, roughly the same every month. Default categories: Housing, Utilities, Insurance, Subscriptions.

Group 2 — Everyday Spending (sort 2): Regular variable spending that happens most weeks. Default categories: Groceries, Dining, Transportation, Personal Care.

Group 3 — Irregular / True Expenses (sort 3): Predictable but infrequent — plan for these monthly even when they do not hit every month (the YNAB true-expenses concept). Default categories: Household, Healthcare, Travel, Gifts, Education, Shopping.

Group 4 — Savings & Goals (sort 4): Money leaving the spending pool for a future purpose. Default categories: Savings.

Group 5 — Just for Fun (sort 5): Discretionary and lifestyle spending. Default categories: Entertainment, Gaming, Vices.

Group 6 — Income (sort 6): Inflows; displayed separately from spending groups. Default categories: Income.

Note: YNAB ships with Immediate Obligations, True Expenses, Debt Payments, Quality of Life Goals, and Just for Fun. Hearth folds Debt Payments into Fixed Monthly (debt is modeled as account types, not categories) and renames Quality of Life Goals to Savings & Goals for clarity.
steps: Navigate to /budget → Categories
Create a group named 'Fixed Monthly'
Drag or assign Housing, Insurance, Subscriptions into that group
Verify the category list renders the group header with categories nested beneath it
Collapse the group and verify the categories hide
Verify uncategorized categories appear under a default 'Other' group
Verify the analytics page respects group order when rendering categories

features.title: Budget targets — monthly envelope per category
features.description: Add a monthly budget target amount to each category (zero-based envelope budgeting). The categories list and analytics page show budgeted vs. actual vs. remaining for the selected month. A category is 'over budget' when actual spending exceeds the target; the delta is highlighted in red. Targets are stored per-category (not per-month) as a default monthly amount, with optional per-month overrides in a budget_targets table keyed by (category_id, year, month).
steps: Navigate to /budget → Categories and open a category
Set a monthly budget target of $500 on Groceries
Navigate to /budget → Analytics for the current month
Verify Groceries shows '$X spent of $500 budgeted' with a progress bar
Add transactions that exceed $500 in Groceries
Verify the category is highlighted as over-budget
Change the month and verify the same $500 target applies

features.title: Ready to assign — unallocated balance indicator
features.description: Show a persistent 'Ready to assign' figure at the top of the budget page: total income for the month minus the sum of all category budget targets. When positive, the user still has dollars without a job. When zero, every dollar is assigned. When negative, they have over-allocated. This is the forcing function for zero-based budgeting — it should be the most prominent number on the page.
steps: Navigate to /budget for the current month
Verify a 'Ready to assign' figure appears at the top
Verify the number equals total income minus sum of all category targets
Set a new category target and verify the figure updates immediately
Reduce a target until 'Ready to assign' reaches $0 and verify a 'fully assigned' state is shown
Over-allocate and verify the figure goes negative with a warning state

features.title: Month-to-month rollover for underspent categories
features.description: Allow a category to be configured to roll over its unspent balance to the next month. If Groceries is budgeted at $500 and only $380 is spent, the following month starts with a $120 carry-forward added to the $500 target. Rollover is opt-in per category (a boolean flag). Overspending in a rollover category reduces the following month's effective budget rather than resetting to zero.
steps: Open a category and enable 'Roll over unspent balance'
End the month with $120 unspent in that category
Navigate to the following month's budget view
Verify the category shows $620 available ($500 target + $120 rollover)
Disable rollover and verify the following month resets to $500
Overspend by $50 with rollover enabled and verify next month shows $450

features.title: Recurring budget transactions
features.description: Mark a transaction as recurring (weekly/monthly) so it auto-appears in future periods. The recurring field follows the same shape as the todos recurring JSONB. A recurring indicator shows on the transaction row.
steps: Open a transaction in the budget import or transactions list
Mark it as recurring with a monthly frequency
Navigate to the next month's transaction list
Verify the transaction appears automatically
Delete the recurrence and verify it stops

features.title: Generate grocery list from recipe ingredients
features.description: On a recipe detail page, a button 'Add to grocery list' opens a picker showing the user's active grocery lists. Selecting one appends the recipe's ingredients as unchecked items.
steps: Navigate to a recipe with ingredients
Click 'Add to grocery list'
Select or create a grocery list from the picker
Navigate to that grocery list
Verify the recipe's ingredients appear as unchecked items
Verify ingredients already on the list are not duplicated

features.title: Show todo due dates on calendar
features.description: Todos with a due_date appear as chips on the corresponding day in the calendar view. Clicking a chip opens the todo sheet. Completed todos appear with a strikethrough.
steps: Create a todo with a due date of tomorrow
Navigate to /calendar
Verify the todo appears as a chip on tomorrow's date
Click the chip and verify the todo sheet opens
Mark the todo complete and verify it shows strikethrough on the calendar

features.title: Show scheduled habits on calendar
features.description: Habits appear on their scheduled days in the calendar view with a distinct visual style (e.g. small dot or chip). Clicking a habit chip opens the habit sheet.
steps: Create a habit scheduled for Monday and Wednesday
Navigate to /calendar and find a Monday
Verify the habit appears on that day
Verify it does not appear on Tuesday
Click the habit indicator and verify the habit sheet opens

features.title: Notification center UI
features.description: A notification bell icon in the shell header showing unread count. Clicking it opens a dropdown/panel listing recent notifications. Notifications can be marked as read. The API domain already exists.
steps: Trigger a notification (e.g. household member completes an assigned todo)
See the bell icon show an unread count badge
Click the bell to open the notification panel
Verify the notification appears with correct message and timestamp
Click 'Mark all read' and verify the badge clears

features.title: Global full-text search across all domains
features.description: The existing command palette (shell) gains a search mode that queries across documents, todos, recipes, contacts, habits, notes, and projects. Results show domain icon, title, and snippet. Selecting a result navigates to that item.
steps: Press Cmd+K to open the command palette
Type a word that appears in a document title
Verify the document appears in results with a document icon
Type a word that appears in a recipe
Verify the recipe appears
Click a result and verify navigation to the correct item

features.title: Email-based household invitations
features.description: Household admins can invite new members by email. The invitee receives an email with a signup link pre-scoped to the household. Currently, new members are added directly (no email flow).
steps: Navigate to Settings → Household
Click 'Invite member' and enter an email address
The invitee receives an email with a join link
Following the link creates an account and joins the household
The new member appears in the member list

features.title: Link goals to projects
features.description: Goals can optionally reference a project. When linked, the goal's progress bar derives from the project's completion percentage. A 'Linked project' field appears in the goal sheet.
steps: Create a goal and open its sheet
Select a linked project from a dropdown
Verify the goal's progress reflects the project's completion
Complete a todo under that project
Verify the goal's progress updates accordingly

features.title: Full data export (household backup)
features.description: A 'Export my data' button in Settings → Account downloads a ZIP file containing all household data as JSON (documents, todos, habits, recipes, etc.) plus any uploaded files.
steps: Navigate to Settings → Account
Click 'Export my data'
A ZIP file downloads
Open the ZIP and verify it contains JSON files for each domain
Verify the document content is present in the exported JSON

features.title: Notification preferences in settings
features.description: Settings → Notifications page where users can toggle which notification types they receive (e.g. todo assignments, habit reminders). Preferences are stored per-member.
steps: Navigate to Settings → Notifications
Toggle off 'Todo assignment notifications'
Have another member assign a todo to you
Verify no notification appears for that event
Toggle it back on and verify notifications resume

features.title: Workout templates
features.description: Save a workout as a template. When logging a new workout, pick from saved templates to pre-fill the exercise list. The templates domain API already exists.
steps: Log a workout with several exercises
Click 'Save as template' and give it a name
Click 'New workout' and select 'From template'
Select the saved template
Verify the exercises are pre-filled in the new workout form

features.title: Notes widget on dashboard
features.description: A new dashboard widget that shows a quick-editable sticky note. Content persists per-widget instance. Useful for household reminders or scratch notes visible at a glance.
steps: Navigate to dashboard
Add a 'Notes' widget via 'Add widget'
Type a note in the widget
Reload the page
Verify the note content persists

features.title: Empty state illustrations for all domain pages
features.description: Every domain list page (todos, habits, recipes, etc.) shows a friendly empty state with a short message and a call-to-action when the list is empty. Currently some pages show a blank area.
steps: Create a new test household with no data
Navigate to /todos — verify a friendly empty state with a 'Create your first todo' button
Navigate to /habits — verify empty state with 'Track your first habit'
Navigate to /recipes — verify empty state with 'Add your first recipe'
Verify each domain page has its own empty state message

features.title: Keyboard shortcuts for common actions
features.description: Add keyboard shortcuts: N = new item on the current domain page, Cmd+K = command palette (already exists), Escape = close any open sheet. Shortcuts are documented in a help modal (? key).
steps: Navigate to /todos and press N
Verify the new-todo sheet opens
Navigate to /habits and press N
Verify the new-habit sheet opens
Press ? and verify a shortcuts reference modal appears
Press Escape to close any open sheet

features.title: Budget profiles — foundation (data model + account assignment)
features.description:
Introduce budget_profiles as a named financial context sitting above the existing personal/household scope. Each household gets two default profiles seeded on creation: 'Personal' and 'Household'. Add profile_id FK to budget_accounts, budget_categories, budget_category_groups, and budget_targets. Backfill existing data: accounts/categories with scope='personal' → Personal profile; scope='shared'/'household' → Household profile. Add a profile switcher dropdown to the budget page header. All existing budget functionality continues to work — this phase is purely additive and invisible to users except for the profile switcher UI.

Key design decisions:
- budget_profiles: (id, household_id, name, budgeting_style ['zero_based'|'profit_tracking'], currency, sort_order, created_at)
- Accounts are assigned to exactly one profile (no many-to-many); cross-profile expenses handled via per-transaction override (budget-011)
- Profile switcher defaults to the last-used profile (stored in localStorage)
- 'Personal' and 'Household' profiles are always present and cannot be deleted
steps: Run migration — verify budget_profiles table exists with Personal and Household rows for the household
Verify all existing accounts have a profile_id pointing at the correct default profile
Verify all existing categories have a profile_id
Open /budget — verify profile switcher dropdown appears in the header
Switch between Personal and Household profiles — verify categories and analytics update to show only that profile's data
Create a new account and verify it prompts for profile assignment
Import a CSV — verify the importer assigns transactions to the selected account's profile

features.title: Budget profiles — scope/profile separation (data model cleanup)
features.description:
Cleanly decouple 'scope' (visibility within a profile) from 'profile' (financial context/attribution). Currently the scope column on transactions conflates both concepts. After profiles exist, scope should mean only: is this transaction private to the owner, or shared with all profile members? Attribution is handled by profile_id (via the account).

Migration: for existing transactions, scope='household' → scope='shared' + profile=Household profile; scope='personal' → scope='private' + profile=Personal profile. Rename scope enum values from 'personal'/'household' to 'private'/'shared' to remove the naming ambiguity. Update all service layer queries, schemas, and frontend references accordingly.

Split logic stays unchanged — split_config and split_override remain on categories and transactions and are resolved against profile members rather than household members.
steps: Run migration — verify existing transactions have correct scope and profile_id after backfill
Verify household-scoped transactions (now scope='shared') are visible to all profile members
Verify personal-scoped transactions (now scope='private') are visible only to the owner
Verify expense splitting still works correctly for shared transactions in the Household profile
Verify the API returns the updated scope values and the frontend renders them correctly
Verify no existing split_config or split_override data was lost

features.title: Budget profiles — cross-profile transaction re-attribution
features.description:
Handle the common case where a transaction imported into one profile actually belongs in another (e.g. Alice pays a shared grocery run on her personal card, but the expense belongs in the Household profile). Add a nullable profile_id override column to budget_transactions. When set, it overrides the account's profile for analytics, categorization, and visibility — the transaction physically stays linked to its account (for balance tracking) but analytically lives in the target profile.

UI: in the transaction row, add a 'Move to profile' action. If the target profile is Household and the category has a split_config, prompt to apply splitting. The original account's balance sheet is unaffected — re-attribution is an analytics concern only.

This is distinct from the reimbursement/transfer pattern (is_transfer=true), which remains available for users who want formal two-sided bookkeeping.
steps: Import a transaction into a Personal profile account
Open the transaction and use 'Move to profile' to re-attribute it to the Household profile
Verify the transaction appears in Household profile analytics but not Personal profile analytics
Verify the source account balance is unaffected
If the target category has a split_config, verify the split prompt appears
Re-attribute back to Personal and verify analytics revert correctly
Verify the existing is_transfer flow still works independently

features.title: Budget profiles — Business profile type with profit-tracking analytics
features.description:
Add a 'Business' profile option with budgeting_style='profit_tracking'. This style presents a P&L view instead of envelope budgeting: revenue streams vs. operating expenses, net profit figure, and a rolling trend chart. The 'Ready to assign' banner is replaced by a 'Net profit' figure (revenue minus expenses). Category groups are pre-seeded for a business context: Revenue, Cost of Goods, Operating Expenses, Owner's Pay.

Creating a Business profile is a paid-tier feature — enforce at the service layer by checking profile count against the household's subscription tier before allowing creation.

Analytics differences for profit_tracking style:
- Primary KPI: net profit = total_income - total_expenses (not 'ready to assign')
- Trend chart: revenue and expenses plotted over rolling 12 months
- No envelope progress bars or rollover (rollover_enabled is ignored for this style)
- Income categories shown as revenue streams with MRR tagging (see budget-013)
steps: Create a new Business profile (paid tier required) — verify it appears in the profile switcher
Verify Business profile shows P&L view: revenue vs expenses, net profit figure
Verify the 'Ready to assign' banner is absent; 'Net profit' shown instead
Add income transactions and expense transactions — verify they appear in correct categories
Verify a rolling 12-month trend chart renders with revenue and expense lines
Attempt to create a Business profile on a free-tier account — verify it is blocked with an upgrade prompt
Verify envelope budgeting (zero_based) still works correctly on Personal and Household profiles

features.title: Budget profiles — MRR tracking for profit-tracking profiles
features.description:
For Business profiles, allow income transactions and categories to be flagged as recurring revenue (MRR). Add a boolean is_recurring_revenue flag to budget_categories (profit_tracking profiles only). When set, the analytics layer aggregates those transactions separately and reports: actual MRR (sum of recurring income this month), projected ARR (MRR × 12), and month-over-month MRR growth.

This pairs with budget-004 (recurring transactions) — a transaction tagged as monthly recurring in a revenue category is an MRR data point. The MRR figure appears as a headline metric on the Business profile dashboard alongside net profit.

Future: MRR chart overlaid with churn/expansion data when customer tracking is added.
steps: Open a Business profile income category and enable 'Recurring revenue (MRR)'
Add monthly recurring income transactions to that category
Verify the Business profile dashboard shows: actual MRR, projected ARR, MoM growth
Add a second recurring revenue category and verify MRR totals aggregate correctly
Navigate to a month with no recurring income — verify MRR shows $0 not null
Disable recurring revenue on a category and verify it drops out of MRR aggregate

features.title: Budget profiles — profile member access control
features.description:
Add explicit per-profile membership with roles, replacing the implicit 'all household members see everything' default. Add a budget_profile_members table: (profile_id, user_id, role ['owner'|'member'|'viewer']). Profiles default to including all household members as 'member' on creation (preserving existing behaviour). Owners can restrict a profile to a subset of household members — useful for a personal side project one partner wants to keep separate, or a business profile with a non-household collaborator (future: external invites).

Role semantics:
- owner: can edit profile settings, manage members, delete profile
- member: can add/edit transactions, categories, targets
- viewer: read-only access to analytics and transactions

Enforce in the service layer: all budget queries already filter by household_id; add profile membership check on top. Phase 1 scope: household members only. External collaborators (non-household members) are explicitly out of scope and deferred.
steps: Open Business profile settings — verify both household members are listed as members
Remove one member from the Business profile
Log in as the removed member — verify the Business profile does not appear in their profile switcher
Add them back as 'viewer' — verify they can see analytics but cannot create/edit transactions
Verify Personal and Household profiles still default to all household members
Verify profile owner cannot remove themselves (must transfer ownership first)

features.title: Transaction search
features.description: Add a search box above the transaction list that filters by description and merchant name (case-insensitive substring match). Also add an 'Uncategorized only' toggle. Both filters work together and reset pagination when changed.
steps: Type a merchant name in the search box — verify only matching transactions appear
Toggle 'Uncategorized only' — verify only transactions with no category appear
Combine search + uncategorized — verify both filters apply simultaneously
Clear search — verify all transactions return
Navigate pages while filtering — verify pagination resets on filter change

features.title: Spending trends chart
features.description: Add a spending trends view to the budget analytics section. Shows the last N calendar months as a grouped bar chart: income (green), expenses (red), budgeted (sky). Hovering a month shows detail. Toggled via a TrendingUp button in the analytics view switcher.
steps: Click the TrendingUp button in the analytics view switcher
Verify the chart shows the last 6 months with income / expense / budgeted bars
Hover a month bar group — verify the detail tooltip shows correct figures
Verify months with no data show zero bars (not absent)
Switch back to pie/list view — verify chart unmounts cleanly

features.title: Account balance field
features.description: Add a manually-maintained current_balance field to budget_accounts. Users enter the balance from their bank statement. Balance is displayed in the account dropdown list and as a clickable chip below the account selector when a specific account is selected. Clicking the chip shows an inline input to update the balance; saving auto-stamps balance_updated_at.
steps: Select a specific account from the dropdown
Verify a 'Set balance' chip appears below the account selector
Click the chip — verify an inline input appears
Enter a balance value and press Enter — verify the chip updates with the formatted balance and date
Open the account dropdown — verify the balance appears alongside the account name
Set a negative balance — verify it displays with a minus sign
Clear the balance (empty input) — verify the chip reverts to 'Set balance'

features.title: Income forecasting — assign next month's money before it arrives
features.description:
A forward-looking monthly allocation view inspired by YNAB's 'Assign Money' workflow. Unlike the current 'Ready to assign' banner (budget-007), which is reactive (actual income received minus targets), income forecasting is proactive: it shows projected income for an upcoming or current month based on recurring income transactions (budget-004), then lets the user allocate that projected amount to envelope categories before transactions arrive.

Core concepts:
- Projected income: sum of all recurring income transactions scheduled for the target month (from budget_transactions where recurring IS NOT NULL and the rule fires in that month). Displayed prominently at the top of the view.
- Allocated: sum of all category budget targets for the target month (same as total_targets in budget-007).
- Left to allocate: projected income minus allocated. Drives the same zero-based discipline as 'Ready to assign' but against forecast rather than actuals.
- When the month has passed, projected income is replaced by actual income and the view becomes a post-hoc reconciliation.

UI approach:
- Add a 'Plan' or 'Forecast' tab/view to the existing budget view switcher (alongside pie/list/envelope/trends).
- Top section: projected income figure (broken down by recurring income source), allocated total, left-to-allocate delta with positive/zero/negative color states.
- Bottom section: the category envelope list (same as envelope view) so the user can adjust targets inline while watching the left-to-allocate figure update in real time.
- Month nav is prominent — this view is most useful looking one month ahead.

API: reuse GET /budget/analytics (already returns total_targets) and add a GET /budget/income-forecast?year=&month=&profile_id= endpoint that returns projected_income (from recurring rules) plus breakdown by source category.
steps: Navigate to /budget and select a future or current month
Click the 'Plan' or 'Forecast' view toggle
Verify projected income is shown based on recurring income transactions for that month
Verify 'Left to allocate' = projected income minus sum of category targets
Adjust a category target inline and verify 'Left to allocate' updates immediately
Set targets that exactly match projected income and verify a 'Fully allocated ✓' state
Over-allocate and verify a negative / warning state
Navigate to a past month and verify projected income is replaced by actual income
Verify months with no recurring income show $0 projected (not null or error)

features.title: Teller bank linking — connect real bank accounts
features.description:
Integrate Teller (teller.io) as the bank connectivity layer for importing transactions and syncing account balances. Teller is developer-friendly with a free tier up to 100 connections, scaling per-connection beyond that — well-suited for self-hosted and early cloud tiers. On the backend, store encrypted Teller enrollment_id and access_token per budget_account. Expose a /budget/accounts/:id/link endpoint that initiates the Teller Connect flow and exchanges the one-time token for a persistent access_token. A background sync job polls Teller for new transactions and upserts them into budget_transactions (deduplicated by teller_transaction_id). Account balances returned by Teller overwrite the manual current_balance field (budget-017) when a sync runs.

Teller supports most US banks and credit unions. Investment accounts (e.g. Vanguard, Fidelity) have variable coverage — verify institution support before surfacing the link option. For unsupported institutions, fall back to CSV import.

This feature is available on all tiers (free and paid) up to the free connection limit; automated daily sync is a paid-tier feature above the free limit.
steps: Navigate to Budget → Accounts and click 'Link account'
Verify the Teller Connect modal opens
Complete the bank auth flow for a supported test institution
Verify a new budget_account is created with the Teller enrollment linked
Verify transactions from the past 30 days are imported and categorized
Verify the account balance chip (budget-017) is populated from Teller's balance response
Trigger a manual sync — verify new transactions appear and duplicates are not created
Disconnect the linked account and verify the access_token is revoked and deleted

features.title: Coinbase crypto portfolio — live asset values (paid tier)
features.description:
Allow users to connect a Coinbase account via OAuth and display their crypto holdings with live USD valuations. This is a paid-tier feature — enforce at the service layer before initiating the OAuth flow.

Integration approach:
- Auth: Coinbase OAuth 2.0 (coinbase.com/oauth/authorize). Store encrypted access_token + refresh_token per budget_account.
- Holdings: GET /v2/accounts returns a list of wallets; filter to non-zero balances.
- Prices: GET /v2/prices/{currency}-USD/spot is a public endpoint (no auth required) — use it to fetch current spot prices for each held asset.
- Display: Each holding shows as an account row: asset name, amount held (e.g. '2.4 ETH'), and current USD value computed as amount × spot price. A total portfolio value figure aggregates all holdings.

Coinbase accounts live in their own profile (separate from Personal/Household bank accounts) or can be added to an existing Business profile. Prices are refreshed on page load; a manual refresh button re-fetches spot prices without a full sync. Holdings are read-only — no write operations to Coinbase are ever made.

Note: crypto asset values are volatile and displayed as indicative only. Do not include them in zero-based envelope budgeting totals by default; show as a separate 'Crypto' section in the account list.
steps: Attempt to connect Coinbase on a free-tier account — verify it is blocked with an upgrade prompt
On a paid-tier account, click 'Link Coinbase account' — verify the OAuth flow opens
Complete OAuth and verify the Coinbase wallets are fetched and stored
Verify each non-zero wallet appears with: asset name, amount held, current USD value
Verify the spot price endpoint is called per asset and values are correctly computed
Verify a total portfolio USD value is displayed across all holdings
Click the manual refresh button — verify spot prices update
Verify Coinbase holdings are isolated from envelope budgeting totals
Revoke Coinbase access and verify tokens are deleted and holdings disappear

features.title: Goal financial linking — spending_cap syncs with budget category
features.description: Goals can be linked to a budget category via financial_link.type=spending_cap. Creating or updating a goal with this link syncs monthly_limit → BudgetCategory.default_monthly_amount. The AI set_category_budget tool syncs back to linked goals. After each Teller sync, check_budget_thresholds fires household notifications at 80% and 100% of any category's monthly target (once per threshold per month).
steps: Create a goal with financial_link={type: spending_cap, category_id: <uuid>, monthly_limit: 500}
Verify the linked BudgetCategory.default_monthly_amount is set to 500
Ask the AI agent to change the dining budget to $400
Verify the goal's financial_link.monthly_limit is updated to 400 and category target matches
Trigger a Teller sync after spending ≥80% of a category budget
Verify a budget_threshold_80 notification appears in the notification bell
Trigger another sync; verify the notification is NOT duplicated
Spend over 100%; verify a budget_threshold_100 notification fires

features.title: User profile + bootstrap pass (Phase 1 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: A persistent, per-user markdown profile that captures who the user is — current focuses, values, recurring patterns, what drains/works, things to not bring up. Stored on the existing member_ai_memory.memory_text column (reused rather than introducing a new user_profiles table — the chatbot was already reading it). The new bits: (1) last_bootstrapped_at column on member_ai_memory so we can tell whether the richer bootstrap pass has run; (2) a new user_profile_updates table holding proposed AI changes that the user accepts or rejects — the AI never silently rewrites the profile via this surface. (3) A bootstrap pass (POST /ai/profile/bootstrap) reads the user's notes, documents, active goals/projects/habits, and the last 90 days of completed todos to draft an initial profile. (4) The coach now loads the profile into its system prompt alongside the existing behavioral context; the chatbot already read memory_text so it benefits automatically. (5) A new 'Profile' subsection in Settings → AI: view current profile, edit directly, run bootstrap, review/accept/reject pending proposals. See docs/ai-coach-redesign.md for the full design.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head; verify last_bootstrapped_at exists on member_ai_memory and user_profile_updates table exists with the documented columns
Navigate to Settings → AI and find the new 'Profile' subsection at the top — for an existing user with empty memory_text it shows 'Your profile is empty' with a 'Build my profile' button
Click 'Build my profile' — bootstrap pass runs, creates a pending user_profile_update with source=bootstrap; the pending card appears below the empty state
Review the proposed profile content; accept it
Verify member_ai_memory.memory_text is populated and last_updated_at advances; the Profile section now renders the accepted content

Trigger a coach digest (POST /ai/coach/digest/generate) — verify the digest reads naturally as if it knows the user (the system prompt now includes a 'What you know about this person' fragment with the profile content)
Open the chat surface and start a new conversation — verify the response is grounded in the profile (e.g. references a current focus or stated preference without name-dropping the profile)
Edit the profile directly via the UI textarea; verify the edit persists (PATCH /ai/profile) and shows in the next coach run
Bootstrap again to create a second pending proposal, then reject it — verify status flips to rejected (POST /ai/profile/updates/{id}/reject returns 204) and memory_text is unchanged
Bootstrap a third time, then accept one of two pending proposals — verify the other still-pending one is automatically marked 'superseded' (so the user never accepts a stale-base proposal by mistake)

features.title: Notes-driven incremental profile proposer (Phase 1.5 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Closes the 'profile goes stale between bootstrap clicks' gap. After every N net-new notes (default 5) created or updated by a user, a background task runs the same proposer prompt as bootstrap but with a smaller, fresher source bundle (the last 15 notes). It either creates a pending UserProfileUpdate (source='incremental') or returns SKIP silently — the system prompt biases hard toward SKIP. Counter `notes_at_last_proposal` lives on member_ai_memory and advances even when the proposer skips, so each user can never trigger more than one proposer call per N new notes. Skips entirely until bootstrap has run at least once — we never silently populate a profile from scratch via the incremental path. Pending updates from this source appear in the same Settings → AI → Profile accept/reject UI as bootstrap proposals. See docs/ai-coach-redesign.md → Phase 1.5.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head; verify notes_at_last_proposal exists on member_ai_memory
As a user who has NOT yet run bootstrap, create 6 new notes; verify NO UserProfileUpdate rows are created (incremental skips until bootstrap has run)
Run bootstrap and accept the proposal; profile is now populated
Create 4 new notes; verify NO proposer call yet (under threshold)
Create the 5th new note; verify a background proposer call fires (logs show 'Incremental proposer' messages) and notes_at_last_proposal advances to the new total
If the proposer found durable signal: a pending UserProfileUpdate with source='incremental' appears in Settings → AI → Profile
If the proposer returned SKIP: no new pending update appears, but notes_at_last_proposal still advanced — verify by adding 5 more notes and watching for the next proposer run
Accept the incremental proposal; verify memory_text is replaced and last_updated_at advances
Verify a note write that fails the proposer call (e.g. AI provider misconfigured) does NOT block the note save — the note commit completes, errors are logged

features.title: Journal signal extraction (Phase 2 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Per-entry structured signal extraction for journal notes. Adds a 'kind' column to collections (kind='journal' identifies journal collections). Migration 0032 backfills via SQL on Postgres. For SQLite (and as a belt-and-suspenders on any backend), an idempotent `backfill_journal_kind` startup hook runs on every API boot (wired into main.py lifespan): tags existing notes-domain collections named 'Journal' or with daily auto_create_rule as kind='journal', and seeds a fresh Journal collection for any household that doesn't have one. `seed_default_journal_collection` is also wired into both signup paths (auth/router.py and setup/router.py) so new households always get one. When a note in a journal-kind collection is saved AND the user has ai_journal_extraction_enabled=true AND a provider is configured, a background asyncio task extracts {sentiment in [-1,+1], self_talk_valence (positive/neutral/harsh/mixed), themes[], notable_phrases[], energy_level, entry_date, extraction_version} and upserts a journal_signals row keyed by note_id. Coach trend helpers (sentiment_trend, harsh_self_talk_streak, dominant_themes_recent) live in journal_signal_service for Phase 3 to use. Opt-out flag added to ai_settings (default true). A POST /ai/journal-signals/backfill endpoint runs synchronous extraction across all existing journal entries — used for first-time bootstraps and after prompt revisions (only_outdated_version=true). UI: new 'Journal signals' subsection in Settings → AI with a toggle and 'Backfill existing entries' button. See docs/ai-coach-redesign.md → Phase 2.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head (Postgres) OR restart the API on SQLite; verify journal_signals table exists, collection.kind exists, ai_settings.ai_journal_extraction_enabled exists (default true)

Check the boot log for 'Journal-kind backfill: tagged=X, seeded=Y, already-tagged=Z' — verify every existing household ends up with at least one collection where kind='journal' after the startup hook runs
Sign up a brand-new account; verify the new household gets a Journal collection seeded automatically via the auth/setup flow (separate from the startup backfill)
In Settings → AI → Journal signals, verify the toggle reads 'On' and the 'Backfill existing entries' button is enabled
Create a new note inside the Journal collection with a meaningful body; verify a journal_signals row appears with non-null sentiment, valence, entry_date, extraction_version=1
Edit that note's content_md; verify the signals row is REPLACED (still one row per note_id, extracted_at advances)
Edit only the title; verify the signals row is NOT re-extracted (title-only changes are skipped)
Run POST /ai/journal-signals/backfill; verify the response shows scanned/extracted/skipped_empty/skipped_current/errors counts and that older journal entries now have signal rows
Toggle 'Extract signals on save' off; create another journal entry; verify NO new signals row is created
Toggle back on; save the same entry again (or create a new one) and verify extraction resumes
Delete a journal note; verify the journal_signals row cascades and is removed
Confirm a non-journal-collection note (e.g. in the default Notes view) does NOT trigger extraction
From a Python shell or test: call journal_signal_service.sentiment_trend / harsh_self_talk_streak / dominant_themes_recent with a recent date; verify the helpers return sensible values
Restart the API a second time; confirm the backfill log line shows tagged=0 seeded=0 (idempotent — no double-seeding)

features.title: CBT-aware coach prompt rewrite (Phase 3 of AI coach redesign)
features.description: Coach prompts rewritten to reason across all three context layers (profile, narrative signals, raw recent entries) plus existing behavioral data. New `_fetch_narrative_context` helper in coach_service pulls sentiment trend (7d vs 30d + delta), harsh-self-talk streak, dominant themes (14-day window, top 5), and the last 5 journal entries verbatim (each capped to 1200 chars). New `_COACH_METHOD_PROMPT` system-prompt fragment, layered ABOVE the tone voice, defines the CBT moves: reality-test harsh self-talk against strong-week data, be honest (not flattering) during real dips, 'bird by bird' register during double-negatives, no manufactured lessons during double-positives, no name-dropping the profile, journal quoting capped at one per response. New `_fmt_narrative` formatter emits a structured 'Recent narrative' section in the user message (or nothing when narrative is empty). Morning/evening/weekly user-message builders all updated to accept and render the narrative block before the briefing instructions. `generate_digest` public signature unchanged — scheduler and on-demand endpoint both continue to work without modification. See docs/ai-coach-redesign.md → Phase 3.
steps: Generate a morning digest in a state where: (a) the last week's todos show a strong completion record AND (b) recent journal entries are harshly self-critical. Verify the coach reality-tests the self-criticism against the data — names what the data shows, doesn't pile on
Generate an evening digest after a real productivity dip + journal entries that sound confident or upbeat. Verify the coach is honest and grounded, not flattering

Generate a digest in a 'both-negative' state (low completion + run of negative journal entries). Verify the response is brief, stays with the user, and offers at most one next step ('bird by bird' register)
Generate a digest in a 'both-positive' state (strong week + positive journal). Verify recognition is proportionate — no manufactured lessons, no inflation

Run digests across all four tones (supportive, stoic, drill_sergeant, gentle_mentor) with the same data. Verify the CBT *method* (reality-testing, gap-noticing) is consistent and only the *voice* changes

Inspect the assembled prompt sent to the provider (e.g. via the AI provider's debug logs). Verify the system prompt contains: tone voice → '## How to coach' → method prompt → profile fragment (if profile exists)
Inspect the user message sent to the provider. Verify it contains a '## Recent narrative' section with sentiment numbers, harsh-streak (if ≥1), themes, and the last few journal entries oldest-first
Verify that when the user has NO journal entries, the narrative section is omitted entirely (no empty header in the prompt)
Verify the coach never name-drops the profile ('I see from your profile…') across 10 sample runs
Verify the coach quotes at most one short journal phrase per response across 10 sample runs

features.title: Context-aware chatbot — read the currently open resource
features.description: The sidebar chatbot now knows what resource the user is currently viewing (note, recipe, document, todo, goal, habit) and can answer questions about 'this' without the user pasting content. New optional `context: {type, id}` field on ChatRequest. Server-side `chat_context_resolver` dispatches by type, enforces visibility/ownership the same way the listing endpoints do (silent fail on unauthorized refs — never breaks chat), and produces a 'What the user is currently viewing' markdown block prepended to the system prompt. Frontend: new CurrentResourceProvider mounted in protected layout, `useRegisterCurrentResource` hook called from notes page (selected note), recipes/[id], and documents/[id]. Chat sidebar reads via `useCurrentResource()`, attaches on every send, and renders a 'Discussing: <title>' chip above the textarea so the magic is visible. Per-resource char caps keep the system prompt small; if the AI needs more it can call the existing domain tools (get_note, get_recipe, etc).
steps: Open a note in the Journal collection; verify the chat sidebar shows 'Discussing: <note title>' chip
Ask the chatbot 'looking at this entry, am I being too hard on myself?' — verify the response is specific to that entry's content (not a generic answer)
Open a recipe; verify the chip updates to 'Discussing: <recipe name>'
Ask 'what could I swap for a spicier version?' — verify the response references the recipe's actual ingredients
Open a document; verify the chip and that the AI knows the document's title/description
Navigate to /todos or /habits (no [id] page); verify the chip disappears and the chatbot reverts to general behavior

Try opening another user's note via a manipulated request (or simulate via API): POST /ai/chat with context.id of a personal note the current user doesn't own → verify the response treats it as if no context was attached (silent failure)
Open a note, navigate to a recipe mid-conversation, then send a new message about 'this' — verify the message resolves to the recipe (current context wins per-message)
Verify the OpenAPI client regenerates cleanly and the frontend tsc check stays at 0 errors

features.title: Coach widget free-text focus field
features.description: Lets the user write 1-2 sentences of free-text guidance for what the coach should focus on, persisted per-widget in AiCoachWidgetConfig.focus and threaded through to /ai/coach/digest/generate. Goes beyond the structured pinned-projects/goals/habits selectors: 'I'm preparing for a hard conversation today, help me think through it' or 'I want to recover from a rough weekend, go easy on productivity nudges' — things the checkboxes can't express. Server renders a '## What I want you to focus on right now' section in the user message just before the briefing instructions, with a directive that this guidance weighs above the default structure. Frontend: new textarea in the AI coach widget settings panel with a 1000-char counter.
steps: Open the AI coach widget settings; find the new 'Focus for this coach' textarea below the pinned-habit selectors
Type a focus string (e.g. 'I have a difficult work conversation today — help me prepare') and trigger Generate / Regenerate
Verify the digest response acknowledges the focus and shapes its content around it (not just generic morning briefing)
Clear the focus textarea; trigger a fresh digest; verify the response goes back to default structure
Test with extreme focus text (>1000 chars): verify the UI caps input at 1000 and the server further trims if needed
Verify focus persists per-widget: configure two coach widgets with different focuses and confirm each digest reflects its own focus
Verify backward compat: a widget config without `focus` field (older saved layouts) still works and behaves as 'no focus' (just the default structure)
Confirm focus is included in the prompt only when non-empty — empty/whitespace-only strings produce no extra prompt section

features.title: Silent profile + auto-bootstrap on API key save + key validation
features.description: Three connected UX changes that turn the AI profile from a user-facing surface into a quiet background service. (1) ProfileSubSection removed from Settings → AI; profile is no longer visible or editable to end users (backend /ai/profile/* endpoints remain for debugging). (2) Bootstrap pass and the notes-driven incremental proposer now write directly to member_ai_memory.memory_text instead of queueing pending updates — the user_profile_updates table is kept as an audit log with status='accepted' on insert. (3) AIProvider gains a validate() method; AnthropicProvider implements it via a max_tokens=1 probe call. PATCH /ai/settings calls validate() before persisting a new key — bad keys return 400 with a clear message rather than silently saving and failing the next chat. (4) On a successful new-key save, /ai/settings fires the bootstrap pass in a background asyncio task if the user has no profile yet (memory_text empty OR last_bootstrapped_at NULL). The user adds their key, and by the time they reach the dashboard the coach already knows them.
steps: In Settings → AI, verify there is NO 'Profile' subsection. The previous user-facing profile UI is gone.

Save a deliberately invalid Anthropic API key (e.g. 'sk-ant-not-a-real-key') — verify the request returns 400 with a clear message ('Invalid API key — Anthropic rejected the credentials') and that ai_settings.api_key_encrypted is NOT updated.
Save a valid Anthropic API key — verify the request succeeds, the key is persisted, and within a few seconds the API log shows the bootstrap pass running for that user.
Wait for the bootstrap task to complete; query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify it now contains a populated H2-sectioned profile (no UI involvement, no clicks).
Trigger a coach digest — verify the response is grounded in the auto-bootstrapped profile.
Open the chat sidebar and start a new conversation — verify the assistant clearly references the profile content in its first response.

Save the SAME key a second time (or rotate to another valid one) when memory_text is already populated — verify no second bootstrap fires (idempotent: memory_text non-empty AND last_bootstrapped_at not NULL).

Write 5+ new journal entries; verify the incremental proposer auto-applies updates directly to memory_text (no pending entries appear in the database; user_profile_updates rows have status='accepted' on insert).
Verify that clearing the key via clear_api_key=true does NOT delete memory_text or audit rows — the profile persists across key rotations.

features.title: Chat-driven profile updates via update_profile tool + coach widget combobox
features.description: Two changes that make the chatbot a first-class profile maintainer and tame the coach widget UI when users have many projects/habits. (1) New `update_profile` chat tool in ai/tools.py: the AI calls it silently (no user prompt) when the user says things like 'focus more on consistency,' 'I've been drinking too much,' 'stop bringing up the old job.' The tool takes a content_md string (full revised profile in the H2-section format) and a diff_summary, writes directly to member_ai_memory.memory_text, and records an audit row. Chat system prompt updated with explicit guidance on when to call (and when NOT — one-off events, transient moods). (2) PinnedSection in the AI coach widget switches to a searchable combobox + chip-list above 8 items per category. Below 8 it keeps the original chip-grid (lowest friction). The combobox is built inline (no Command/Popover primitive in the project) — text input + filtered list popover + outside-click dismiss.
steps: Open the chat sidebar and say 'I want to focus more on consistency and showing up daily moving forward.' Verify the assistant acknowledges in plain language (e.g. 'Got it, I'll keep that in mind') WITHOUT mentioning the tool or the profile.
Query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify the profile has been updated to include the new focus, with existing sections preserved.
Verify a row exists in user_profile_updates with source='manual', status='accepted', and resolved_at set.
Trigger a fresh coach digest — verify the response reflects the new focus.
Say something transient like 'I had a bad day at work today.' Verify the assistant responds empathetically but does NOT call update_profile (single-event content shouldn't bloat the profile).
Configure a coach widget when you have FEW projects/goals/habits (≤8 of each) — verify the settings panel still shows the original chip-grid layout.
Configure a coach widget when you have MANY projects (>8) — verify the Focus Projects section now shows a combobox with selected chips above and a 'Pick from N' button.
Click the 'Pick from N' button; verify the popover opens with an autofocused search input and a filtered scrollable list. Type to filter; click an item to add it as a chip. Click again to deselect.
Click outside the popover; verify it closes. Re-open and confirm state is preserved.

features.title: Profile versioning + weekly scheduled refresh + decay (Phase 4)
features.description: Final phase of the AI coach redesign. Three connected pieces. (1) Migration 0034 adds the user_profile_versions append-only history table. A new _apply_profile_update helper in profile_service is the single write path for memory_text — bootstrap, incremental proposer, the update_profile chat tool, direct PATCH /ai/profile, and the new scheduled refresh all flow through it. It snapshots the previous content into user_profile_versions, then overwrites memory_text, then trims rows beyond PROFILE_VERSION_RETENTION (50) for that user. ProfileUpdateSource literal expanded to include 'scheduled' and 'direct_edit'. (2) New run_scheduled_profile_refresh function does two jobs: integrate durable patterns from the last 4 weeks (notes + recent chat user messages) AND decay stale content from the current profile (things that haven't recurred, resolved past situations, fixed-trait language, recent contradictions). The proposer prompt is biased hard toward SKIP — a profile that's currently accurate IS the correct weekly outcome. (3) New APScheduler weekly job at Sunday 03:00 iterates every user with a non-empty profile, resolves their provider, runs the refresh, logs aggregate counts. Each per-user iteration wrapped in its own try/except. (4) New GET /ai/profile/versions?limit=N endpoint for debug — newest-first history, no UI. The profile stops being a write-only blob that grows monotonically — over weeks of use, stale focuses get pruned and stable new patterns get integrated without anyone clicking anything.
steps: Run alembic upgrade head (Postgres) OR restart the API on SQLite; verify user_profile_versions table exists with the documented columns and the (user_id, created_at) index
Save a new API key to trigger bootstrap; query user_profile_versions — verify NO row was created (snapshotting the empty old content is correctly skipped)
After bootstrap, edit the profile via PATCH /ai/profile with new content_md; query user_profile_versions — verify ONE row exists with source='direct_edit' and the OLD bootstrap content_md
Trigger the update_profile chat tool ('focus more on consistency'); verify a SECOND version row appears with source='manual' and the previous direct-edit content
Add 5 journal entries to trigger the incremental proposer; if it applies (not SKIP), verify a version row appears with source='incremental'
Call GET /ai/profile/versions?limit=10 — verify it returns the version history newest-first with all the right sources
Restart the API; verify the boot log shows the scheduler started with 'profile_refresh=Sun 03:00' in the timing line

Manually invoke run_scheduled_profile_refresh_all() from a Python shell — verify the response shows {users, applied, skip, skipped_no_activity, error} counts, and that users with no profile or no activity are correctly skipped

Force a scheduled refresh that finds enough new material to land an update; verify (a) the new memory_text is applied, (b) a user_profile_versions row was added with source='scheduled', (c) a user_profile_updates audit row was added with source='scheduled' status='accepted'
Insert 60 mock version rows for a user; trigger another _apply_profile_update; verify the count is trimmed back to 50 (PROFILE_VERSION_RETENTION) — oldest rows deleted
Verify PROFILE_UPDATE_SOURCES constant rejects unknown sources: _apply_profile_update(..., source='garbage') should raise ValueError
Confirm the scheduled refresh prompt biases toward SKIP: run it against a user with a healthy, current profile and minimal new activity → outcome should be 'skip' (no version row, no update row)

features.title: Guided journaling tool — Talk-it-out sessions with personalized openers
features.description: Adds a 'Talk it out' button to today's journal entry that launches a full-screen guided journal session. Adaptive AI register: matches the user's energy — rant mode gets brief acknowledgements and no questions, reflective mode gets focused questions, never starts with a question, never gives advice unsolicited. When the user wrestles with anything interpersonal, the AI gently points at the people in their life (not as a disclaimer, naturally). Role-clarity baked in: 'You are NOT a therapist, NOT a friend, NOT a substitute for the people in this person's life.' Phase B: the AI OPENS the session with a personalized 1-2 sentence message that draws on profile + sentiment trend + harsh-self-talk streak + dominant themes + time of day. Opener never ends with a sharp question — it opens the space without directing it. Static fallback openers cover provider failures. On Finish, the AI synthesizes a first-person summary; user reviews/edits, then saves. Optional 'Save with transcript' toggle appends the full conversation below a `---` divider so the summary leads but the working session is preserved. The saved content appends to today's existing journal entry (preserving any prior Write-mode content) so one day = one journal entry. Backend: AiConversation.kind ('chat'|'journal') + note_id columns (migration 0035); journal-mode system prompt + synthesis prompt + opener prompt in service.py; three new endpoints (POST /ai/journal/start, /ai/journal/{id}/finish, /ai/journal/save). /ai/journal/start now returns opening_message and persists it as the first assistant turn for new sessions. Chat streaming honors conversation.kind — journal sessions use the journal-mode prompt and only the update_profile tool is exposed (no task tools). Frontend: new JournalSession component (~370 lines) renders a full-screen overlay reusing the existing /ai/chat SSE; renders the opener as the first assistant message; new isTodayJournalEntry helper gates the 'Talk it out' button by parsing entry_date from the title with fallback to created_at. NoteResponse gains collection_kind so the frontend can gate cleanly without an extra collection lookup.
steps: Restart the API (SQLite auto-patch) OR run alembic upgrade head (Postgres) — confirm ai_conversations has kind + note_id columns and the (user_id, kind, note_id) index
Run `npm run codegen` in web/ to regenerate schema.d.ts against the running API (the manual patch I applied is a placeholder)
Open today's journal entry — verify the 'Talk it out' button appears in the tags row, with a Sparkles icon
Open YESTERDAY's journal entry (or any past one) — verify the button does NOT appear (strict-today gating)
Open a non-journal note (regular notes collection) — verify the button does NOT appear (collection_kind gating)
Click 'Talk it out' on today's entry — full-screen overlay opens and the AI's personalized opener appears as the first assistant message before you type anything
Phase B opener check (1): with an empty profile and no journal history, verify the opener falls back gracefully (warm + non-directive, e.g. 'I'm here. Take your time.')

Phase B opener check (2): after a few days of journaling, open a fresh session — verify the opener references something concrete from recent narrative (a dominant theme, a softer tone if there's been harsh self-talk, time-of-day appropriate framing)

Phase B opener check (3): verify the opener does NOT end with a sharp question — open-ended invitations only ('Take your time', 'I'm here', 'What's on your mind' are OK; 'How was your meeting?' is NOT)
Phase B opener check (4): verify the opener is persisted as the FIRST assistant message in the conversation — re-open the session (resume path) and confirm subsequent /ai/chat turns build on it
Send a message; verify the AI streams back a brief, NON-question response (rant-mode register)
Test rant register: vent for 2-3 messages straight; verify the AI keeps responses short and doesn't pile on questions/advice
Test reflective register: say something planning-oriented ('I'm trying to think through tomorrow'); verify the AI shifts to focused-question mode
Test point-at-humans rule: say 'I had a fight with my partner and don't know what to say'; verify the AI mentions talking to the partner (not as a disclaimer, naturally)

Test update_profile silently: say 'I want to focus more on showing up daily moving forward'; verify the AI acknowledges in plain language ('got it, I'll keep that in mind') and the profile updates (curl /ai/profile to confirm)

Verify NO other tools are exposed in journal mode — saying 'create a habit for exercise' should NOT trigger a create_habit call (the AI should respond conversationally, possibly point at doing it after the session)
Click 'Finish' after 3+ exchanges; verify the AI synthesizes a first-person 'I' summary (not third person), and renders it in an editable textarea
Edit the summary slightly; toggle OFF 'Save with transcript'; click Save → verify the journal note's content_md now has the summary appended (with a `---` divider only if there was prior content)

Start a fresh session on the same entry (or another day's entry); toggle ON 'Save with transcript'; verify the saved content contains the summary, then `---`, then `## Conversation transcript`, then the full back-and-forth in 'You:' / 'Coach:' format
Re-open the same day's entry and click 'Talk it out' again — verify the session RESUMES (POST /ai/journal/start returns the existing conversation_id with is_new=false)
Click 'Don't save this session' from the review phase — verify the modal closes without changing the journal entry
Confirm the on-save flow triggers the existing signal extractor — query journal_signals after save to verify a row appears for the updated note
Confirm the saved entry feeds the next coach digest — generate a coach digest after saving and verify the narrative section references the entry's content

features.title: Rate limiting on auth endpoints
features.description: Brute-force and spam protection on public auth endpoints using slowapi (in-memory storage, single-process). Key function respects X-Forwarded-For and X-Real-IP for correct behaviour behind Caddy (self-hosted) and Vercel (cloud). Limits: POST /auth/login — 5/minute per IP; POST /auth/register — 3/hour per IP. Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. Note: in-memory storage is correct for local and single-process self-hosted. When deploying to a multi-worker cloud environment, swap MemoryStorage for RedisStorage in core/rate_limit.py.
steps: Start the API and send 6 rapid POST /auth/login requests from the same IP
Verify the 6th request returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests
Verify the response includes a Retry-After header
Wait 1 minute and verify login works again
Send 4 POST /auth/register requests within an hour
Verify the 4th returns HTTP 429
Verify legitimate login from a different IP is unaffected during a lockout

features.title: Encryption at rest — managed Postgres (cloud deployment checklist)
features.description: When moving to a managed Postgres provider (Neon, Supabase, AWS RDS, etc.), enable encryption at rest as a provider-level checkbox. This is not a code change — it's a deploy-time configuration step. Protects against physical disk theft and raw DB dumps (Threat 1). All managed providers support it; it should be verified as enabled on day one of any cloud deployment. Combined with field-level encryption (security-001), this means sensitive data is protected both at the storage layer and the application layer.
steps: Confirm encryption at rest is enabled in the managed Postgres provider's settings before accepting any real user data
Document the setting location in infra/CLAUDE.md for the cloud deployment tier
Verify SSL/TLS is enforced for all connections to the DB (no plaintext connections permitted)

features.title: Field-level encryption for sensitive database columns (Phase 1 + 2)
features.description: Fernet-based (AES-128-CBC + HMAC-SHA256) field-level encryption via SQLAlchemy TypeDecorators that transparently encrypt on write and decrypt on read at the ORM boundary. No service-layer changes required. Protects against DB breach and server compromise (Threats 1 & 2). Phase 1 — active credentials: BudgetAccount.teller_access_token (live bank access token), AiSettings.api_key_encrypted (BYOK API key). Phase 2 — sensitive personal data: MemberAiMemory.memory_text (AI psychological profile), AiCoachDigest.content (daily coaching insights), UserProfileUpdate.proposed_content_md, UserProfileVersion.content_md, JournalSignal.notable_phrases (EncryptedJSON, column changed JSON→TEXT). Key managed via FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY env var; comma-separated keys supported for zero-downtime rotation via MultiFernet. Graceful degradation when key not set (local dev). Migration 0036 handles the schema change (notable_phrases JSON→TEXT) and encrypts all existing plaintext rows.
steps: Verify FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY is set in api/.env
Run make migrate (or alembic upgrade head on Postgres) — confirm 0036 applies cleanly
Save a BYOK API key via Settings → AI; confirm AI chat still works (key is encrypted on write, decrypted on read transparently)
Link a bank account via Teller; confirm sync still works (teller_access_token encrypted in DB, decrypted transparently when used)
Directly query the DB (e.g. SELECT api_key_encrypted FROM ai_settings) — verify the stored value is a Fernet ciphertext (starts with 'gAAAAA'), not the plaintext key
Directly query journal_signals.notable_phrases — verify stored values are Fernet ciphertext, not JSON arrays
Directly query member_ai_memory.memory_text — verify stored value is ciphertext, not plaintext markdown
Restart the API — verify no errors on boot; encrypted values still decrypt correctly after restart
Test key rotation: prepend a new Fernet key to FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY (comma-separated); restart API; verify existing encrypted values still decrypt correctly with the old key

features.title: Zero-knowledge / E2EE opt-in for cloud tier
features.description: Opt-in end-to-end encryption for users who want the strongest privacy guarantee. When enabled, the most sensitive fields (AI profile, journal content) are encrypted client-side before upload using a key derived from the user's password (Argon2 → symmetric key). The server stores only ciphertext it cannot read. Trade-offs: (1) password recovery requires saved recovery codes — traditional 'forgot password' email reset is impossible; (2) server-side AI features (coach digest generation, journal signal extraction) cannot operate on E2EE fields without client-side decryption first — user may need to consent to a 'decrypt → send to AI → re-encrypt' flow; (3) household shared data requires a household key encrypted separately with each member's derived key. UX: onboarding step offering 'Standard' vs 'Private mode' with an explainer; also togglable in Settings → Privacy with sufficient warnings. Best set at account creation — toggling on later requires re-encrypting all existing data client-side. Recovery codes generated and downloaded at setup. Cloud-tier only (self-hosted users already control their own keys).
steps: During onboarding, verify a privacy mode selection step appears with clear Standard vs Private mode explanation
Selecting Private mode triggers recovery code generation and download
User must confirm they have saved recovery codes before proceeding
Verify AI coach features show a 'requires decryption' prompt when E2EE is active
Verify forgot-password flow is replaced by a recovery code flow for E2EE users
Verify a second household member can read shared data encrypted with the household key
Verify toggling E2EE on in Settings re-encrypts existing data client-side
Verify toggling E2EE off decrypts all data and stores plaintext server-side

features.title: Per-member AI access control
features.description: Households are multi-member by design and parents will eventually add kids. This feature gives household admins a per-member toggle that decides whether each account has access to AI features (coach, chat, journal, profile personalization). Defaults TRUE so existing members are unaffected. Backend: migration 0036 adds ai_features_enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE to household_memberships; HouseholdMembership model + MemberResponse + UserResponse all expose it; new require_ai_enabled FastAPI dependency raises 403 with a clear message ('AI features are disabled for your account by your household admin') and is applied to all 22 /ai/* endpoints; new PATCH /households/members/{user_id} endpoint for admins to flip the toggle (admins cannot disable their OWN AI through this endpoint to avoid lockout — guardrail returns 400 with a hint to clear their API key in Settings → AI instead). The flag is attached to current_user in get_current_user and returned on /auth/me so the frontend doesn't need a separate round-trip. Frontend: new MemberRow component in Settings → Household — admins see a real AI toggle per row (their own row is disabled with a tooltip explaining why); non-admins see a read-only 'AI: on/off' status pill. When the current user's ai_features_enabled is false: the AI tab in Settings is hidden, the AI coach widget on the dashboard shows a quiet disabled state ('AI features are disabled for your account'), the sidebar Ask-AI icon is hidden, the ⌘K keyboard shortcut is no-op, the AI chat panel won't open, and the 'Talk it out' button on today's journal entry doesn't render. Existing AI data (profile, journal_signals, audit logs) is NOT wiped when the flag flips — disabling is a posture toggle. Flipping back to TRUE restores the full experience with all prior data intact.
steps: Restart the API (SQLite auto-patch) OR run alembic upgrade head (Postgres); verify household_memberships has ai_features_enabled BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT TRUE
GET /auth/me — verify the response includes ai_features_enabled: true
GET /households/members — verify each row includes ai_features_enabled: true
As an admin, PATCH /households/members/{another_user_id} with {"ai_features_enabled": false} — verify 200, then GET /households/members and confirm the change persisted
As an admin, attempt PATCH /households/members/{your_own_user_id} with ai_features_enabled=false — verify 400 with the guardrail message

As the now-disabled member, GET /ai/profile — verify 403 with the clear detail message ('AI features are disabled for your account by your household admin. Ask an admin to enable them in Settings → Household.')
As the disabled member, GET /ai/coach/digest — verify 403; POST /ai/journal/start — verify 403; POST /ai/chat — verify 403; all 22 /ai/* endpoints uniformly return 403

As the disabled member in the web app: verify the AI tab in Settings sidebar is hidden; the AI coach widget on dashboard shows the disabled-state placeholder; the sidebar Ask-AI icon is hidden; pressing ⌘K does nothing; the chat panel can't be opened; today's journal entry shows no 'Talk it out' button

As an admin, re-enable the member by toggling ai_features_enabled back to true — verify all UI surfaces and endpoints come back fully functional with previously-stored profile/journal_signals/audit history intact
Verify the toggle on a non-admin's own row in the Household members list is rendered but disabled (with tooltip pointing them to clear their API key for self-disable)
Verify a non-admin viewing the Household members list sees the AI on/off status as a read-only pill (no toggle they can modify)

Confirm AI data was NOT deleted during the disable/enable cycle — query member_ai_memory, user_profile_versions, journal_signals, user_profile_updates and verify rows for the disabled member are still present

features.title: Per-user scoping for behavioral data in coach + bootstrap
features.description: Audit found that personal text data (notes, journal entries, journal signals, AI conversations, profile) was correctly user-scoped, but the behavioral data fetchers used by the coach digest and profile bootstrap were filtering by household_id only — meaning a household partner's todos, habits, goals, and projects would land in your coach context and your profile bootstrap. Fixed by adding per-user filters: Todo.assigned_to_user_id == user_id for todo queries; Habit.created_by_user_id == user_id for habits; Goal.created_by_user_id == user_id for goals; Project.created_by_user_id == user_id for projects. Applied to coach_service._fetch_context (5 todo queries + habits + goals + projects), coach_service._fetch_history (added user_id parameter; weekly_completions + habit query), and profile_service._gather_bootstrap_sources (all 4 behavioral queries). Side note surfaced during verification: _fetch_history uses Postgres-only date_trunc, so the 6-week trajectory block silently fails on SQLite — separate bug, tracked as a follow-up.
steps: Restart the API; load the AI coach widget and generate a morning digest
Verify the digest's 'Completed yesterday' list does NOT include todos assigned to other household members
Verify habit lines in the digest only reference habits this user created
Verify the digest's goal-progress block (if shown) only shows goals this user created
Verify the digest's active-projects list (if shown) only shows projects this user created

Trigger a profile bootstrap (Settings → AI → save key, or manual /ai/profile/bootstrap) and confirm the proposer prompt only references this user's behavioral data — no partner's todos/habits/goals/projects in the source-material section
(Multi-user setup only): create a partner user in the same household, seed a few of their todos/habits/goals/projects, then regenerate this user's digest and verify clean separation

features.title: Talk-it-out check-in modes (Mood, Body, Rant, Day review)
features.description: Brandon flagged that 'Talk it out' starts with a blank slate, which can feel daunting. Added pre-built check-in modes the user picks before the conversation begins, layered on top of a blank-slate-always-available default. Modes: Mood check ('what's the first word that comes up'), Body check (somatic anchor), Rant (vent — system prompt tells the AI to stay with the user, NOT reality-test), Day review (looks AHEAD before 4pm local / BACK after 4pm). Each mode (a) seeds a canned opening message (zero latency, no LLM call) and (b) appends a mode-specific instruction block to the journal system prompt so the model adapts its register. Backend: migration 0037 adds ai_conversations.mode (VARCHAR(32) NULL); _MODE_OPENERS + canned_opener_for(mode, local_hour) helper in ai/service.py; _MODE_INSTRUCTIONS dict layered onto _build_journal_system_prompt; /ai/journal/start now accepts mode + local_hour, handles three call patterns (mount-no-mode, mode-pick, resume), and returns needs_mode_pick so the frontend doesn't need to derive it. Mode is locked once chosen — repeated /start calls with a different mode are ignored. The LLM-generated opener from journal-001 Phase B is no longer invoked (kept in code as _JOURNAL_OPENER_PROMPT in case we want it back later). Frontend: JournalSession renders 4 structured chips + a small 'or start with a blank slate' link above the empty conversation. Picker visibility is driven by the backend's needs_mode_pick flag. Each chip clicked → fast canned opener, picker disappears. Resumed sessions skip the picker.
steps: Restart the API (SQLite auto-patch handles 0037 on startup; Postgres: alembic upgrade head)
Open Talk-it-out on today's journal entry — verify the 4 structured mode chips appear above the textarea with one-line hints
Verify the 'or start with a blank slate' link is rendered below the chips
Click 'Mood check' — verify the canned opener appears within ~200ms (no LLM latency) and the picker disappears
Type a reply — verify the AI's response stays short and feeling-focused (no reality-testing, no problem-solving)
Open a different day's journal entry and pick 'Rant' — type a complaint and verify the AI stays with you (no reality-test, no reframe, no human-pointing — the rant IS the release)
Pick 'Day review' in the morning (before 4pm local) — verify the opener asks 'Looking ahead at today...'
Pick 'Day review' in the evening (after 4pm local) — verify the opener asks 'What's standing out from today...'
Pick 'Body check' — verify the opener asks about tension/energy/tiredness, not feelings directly
Click 'blank slate' link — verify the picker disappears immediately with no opener, you can just start typing
Close the modal without picking a mode, then re-open it — verify the picker re-appears (conversation is still empty)
Close the modal AFTER picking a mode and typing something — verify reopening goes straight to the conversation, no picker
Confirm the chosen mode persists across reloads (refresh the page mid-session, reopen — mode is still locked)

features.title: Field-level encryption Phase 3 — evaluate notes, messages, transactions, contacts
features.description: Decision point: evaluate whether and how to encrypt the remaining high-volume content fields. Fields in scope: notes.content_md + notes.content_json (journal/note bodies), ai_messages.content (chat history), budget_transactions.description (raw bank descriptions), contacts PII (email, phone, address). The blocker is search: these fields either have active full-text search (notes, messages) or LIKE-based filtering (transactions, contacts), and naive Fernet encryption breaks both. Options to evaluate before implementing: (1) Drop search on encrypted fields — acceptable if users don't rely on it; (2) Blind index — store HMAC(key, normalized_value) alongside ciphertext for exact/prefix search; works well for transactions and contacts, not for full-text; (3) Separate search index table — keep a tsvector index populated at write time, encrypt the content column; attacker sees topics but not full text; (4) Encrypt journal messages only — ai_messages has a kind column ('chat' vs 'journal'); journal conversations are far more sensitive and not searched. Recommended: decide alongside the design of search-001 (global full-text search) so the search architecture and encryption strategy are co-designed rather than retrofitted against each other.
steps: Review search-001 design — determine whether a server-side tsvector or client-side search index is the intended approach
Decide on blind index vs separate search index vs no-search for each field group
Encrypt budget_transactions.description and contacts PII (blind index approach, no full-text needed)
Decide on ai_messages.content strategy — drop search, encrypt journal-kind only, or separate index
Decide on notes.content_md strategy — this is the highest-impact call since notes are the primary content domain
Implement chosen approach and verify search still works as designed

features.title: Transaction data retention — purge raw transactions, keep monthly aggregates
features.description: Data minimization: raw bank transaction descriptions are sensitive (reveal merchants, habits, health patterns) and don't need to be kept forever. After a configurable retention window (default 24 months), purge raw BudgetTransaction rows and keep only monthly category aggregates. Requires: (1) a new BudgetMonthlySummary model (year, month, category_id, profile_id, total_amount) seeded from existing aggregate queries; (2) a backfill migration to populate it from existing transactions; (3) a purge_old_transactions service function that verifies each month is covered in the summary table before deleting; (4) a periodic background job (APScheduler) that runs the purge on a schedule; (5) a user-configurable retention_months field on BudgetProfile (default 24, options: 12/24/60/forever). The aggregate data was already being computed on the fly for analytics — this materializes it so raw data can be safely dropped. Users who upload historical imports and want to keep them can set retention to 'forever'. Note: the aggregation queries already exist in budget/service.py (get_summary, etc.) — this feature materializes those results and adds the deletion layer on top.
steps: Run migration — verify budget_monthly_summaries table exists with (year, month, category_id, profile_id, total_amount) columns
Verify the backfill populates a summary row for every (year, month, category) combination that has existing transactions
Verify the analytics views still return correct figures after the backfill (queries now hit the summary table for historical months)
Set retention to 12 months on a test profile; trigger the purge job manually
Verify raw transactions older than 12 months are deleted and the summary rows are intact
Verify raw transactions within the 12-month window are NOT deleted
Navigate to /budget analytics for a purged month — verify it still shows correct totals from the summary table
Set retention to 'forever' and verify the purge job skips that profile entirely
Verify the background job runs on schedule and logs how many rows it purged

features.title: Warrant canary (cloud tier)
features.description: A publicly visible statement on the Hearth cloud service status/about page stating 'We have never received a National Security Letter, gag order, or secret court order.' The statement is updated (or removed) with each audit period. If Hearth ever receives such an order, the canary is silently removed rather than explicitly confirmed — users who watch for its presence are informed without Hearth violating a gag order. Low-cost, no backend work required — just a public page with a dated statement and a commitment to keep it current. Relevant only for the cloud-hosted tier. Self-hosted users control their own infrastructure and have no Hearth trust assumption. Pairs with the ToS commitment to never sell user data.
steps: Add a warrant canary statement to the cloud product's public status or legal page
Date the statement and commit to a review cadence (e.g. quarterly)
Document internally what the removal procedure looks like if ever needed
